merged_events.event_description: Diplomatic relations established and the American Legation in Belgrade opened when U.S. Consul General Eugene Schuyler presented his credentials to the Serbian government, though Schuyler was resident at Athens.

evidence.quote: Diplomatic relations between the U.S. and Serbia were established on November 10, 1882, when U.S. Consul General Eugene Schuyler presented his credentials to the government of Serbia. At this time, the American Legation in Belgrade was established, though Schuyler was resident at Athens.

merged_events.event_description: The American Legation moved to London following the Yugoslav government going into exile in England. Anthony J. Drexel Biddle Jr. presented credentials as U.S. Minister Near the Government of Yugoslavia in England, resident in London.

evidence.quote: Anthony J. Drexel Biddle Jr. presented his credentials as the U.S. Minister Near the Government of Yugoslavia in England on October 3, 1941, and was resident in London.

merged_events.event_description: The American Legation Belgrade was formally raised to Embassy status with the appointment of Anthony J. Drexel Biddle Jr. as Ambassador Extraordinary and Plenipotentiary.

evidence.quote: The American Legation Belgrade was raised to the status of Embassy with the appointment of Anthony J. Drexel Biddle Jr. as Ambassador Extraordinary and Plenipotentiary on September 29, 1942.

merged_events.event_description: The U.S. Embassy followed the Yugoslav government-in-exile to Egypt. Lincoln MacVeagh presented credentials as U.S. Ambassador Near the Government of Yugoslavia Established in Egypt.

evidence.quote: On September 28, 1943, the Government of Yugoslavia was transferred to Egypt. Lincoln MacVeagh, the U.S. Ambassador Near the Government of Yugoslavia Established in Egypt, presented his credentials on December 9, 1943.

merged_events.event_description: The U.S. Embassy followed the Yugoslav government-in-exile back to England. Rudolf E. Schoenfeld was designated U.S. Chargé d'Affaires ad interim near the Government of Yugoslavia Established in England, later succeeded by Ambassador Patterson.

evidence.quote: On July 1, 1944, Rudolf E. Schoenfeld was designated U.S. Chargé d'Affaires ad interim near the Government of Yugoslavia Established in England. On November 17, 1944, Richard Cunningham Patterson presented his credentials as U.S. Ambassador near the Government of Yugoslavia Established in England.

merged_events.event_description: Embassy Belgrade returned to Yugoslavia and resumed operations in Belgrade as the Yugoslav government returned from exile near the end of the war.

evidence.quote: On March 31, 1945, Embassy Belgrade returned to Yugoslavia and resumed operations in Belgrade. Richard Cunningham Patterson continued to serve as U.S. Ambassador to Yugoslavia and was resident in Belgrade.

merged_events.event_description: The U.S. announced it would not recognize the Federal Republic of Yugoslavia as a successor state to the SFRY. Ambassador Zimmerman was recalled, and the U.S. took the position that the SFRY had ceased to exist, effectively terminating diplomatic relations.

evidence.quote: On May 21, 1992, the U.S. announced that it would not recognize the Federal Republic of Yugoslavia (FRY) as a successor state of the Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia (SFRY). The FRY was composed of the Republics of Serbia and Montenegro. U.S. Ambassador Warren Zimmerman was recalled from Belgrade, but the mission continued with a staff under the authority of a Chargé d'Affaires ad interim.

merged_events.event_description: Henry Percival Dodge presented credentials as Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary to the Kingdom of the Serbs, Croats, and Slovenes, establishing a U.S. Legation.

evidence.quote: Henry Percival Dodge, Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary. Appointed: 1919-07-17. Credentials presented: 1919-10-05. Left post on 1926-03-21. Note: Commissioned to the Kingdom of the Serbs, Croats, and Slovenes.

merged_events.event_description: Anthony Joseph Drexel Jr Biddle was promoted from Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary to Ambassador Extraordinary and Plenipotentiary, elevating the U.S. mission near the Yugoslav government-in-exile in England from a Legation to an Embassy.

evidence.quote: Anthony Joseph Drexel Jr Biddle, Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary. Appointed: 1941-07-30. Credentials presented: 1941-10-03. Promoted to Ambassador Extraordinary and Plenipotentiary 1942-10-03. Note: Served near the Government of Yugoslavia established in England.

merged_events.event_description: Embassy Belgrade was closed, ending U.S. diplomatic representation in Yugoslavia.

evidence.quote: Richard Monroe Miles, Chargé d'Affaires ad interim. Credentials presented: 1996-08. Embassy Belgrade closed on 1999-03-23. Note: Embassy personnel left Serbia-Montenegro on March 24, 1999.

matched.notes: Exact match. POCOM confirms Henry Percival Dodge presented credentials on 1919-10-05 to the Kingdom of the Serbs, Croats, and Slovenes, establishing the resident Legation.

RDCR (Event 2) gives 1942-09-29 as the date the Legation was raised to Embassy; POCOM (Event 8) gives 1942-10-03 as the promotion date. Per source priority, RDCR's explicit institutional date of 1942-09-29 is correct, matching the CSV. The POCOM date reflects the personnel promotion date.

missing_from_csv.event_description: The American Legation moved to London following the Yugoslav government going into exile in England. Biddle presented credentials as U.S. Minister Near the Government of Yugoslavia in England on October 3, 1941.
missing_from_csv.notes: This represents the Legation relocating from Belgrade to London with the government-in-exile. Could be coded as a location change event. The mission continued as a Legation but was now near a government-in-exile.

missing_from_csv.event_description: The U.S. Embassy followed the Yugoslav government-in-exile to Egypt. Lincoln MacVeagh presented credentials on December 9, 1943.
missing_from_csv.notes: Embassy relocation to Egypt with the government-in-exile. Not a status change (still Embassy) but a significant location/operational change.

missing_from_csv.event_description: The U.S. Embassy followed the Yugoslav government-in-exile back to England. Schoenfeld designated Chargé d'Affaires ad interim.
missing_from_csv.notes: Embassy relocation back to England. Citation error flagged — treat with extra skepticism. Not a status change but a location change.

missing_from_csv.event_description: Embassy Belgrade returned to Yugoslavia and resumed operations in Belgrade as the Yugoslav government returned from exile.
missing_from_csv.notes: Embassy returned to Belgrade from exile. Not a status change but marks the resumption of normal operations in-country.

missing_from_csv.event_description: The U.S. announced it would not recognize the Federal Republic of Yugoslavia as a successor state to the SFRY. Ambassador Zimmerman was recalled. The U.S. took the position that the SFRY had ceased to exist.
missing_from_csv.notes: Significant event: effective termination of recognition of Yugoslavia as successor state. However, RDCR notes the mission continued under a Chargé d'Affaires, so this may not be a full 'None' status — it could be an Interests section or downgraded presence. The CSV may intentionally end its Serbia/Yugoslavia timeline here or continue it under a different country entry.

missing_from_csv.event_description: Embassy Belgrade was closed; embassy personnel left Serbia-Montenegro on March 24, 1999.
missing_from_csv.notes: POCOM source confirms Embassy closure. This may be tracked under a separate country entry (e.g., Federal Republic of Yugoslavia or Serbia-Montenegro) rather than the Kingdom of Serbia/Yugoslavia CSV.

discrepancies.reasoning: The RDCR text explicitly states: 'the American Legation in Belgrade was established' on November 10, 1882, which supports 'Legation' as the institutional status. However, it also states Schuyler 'was resident at Athens,' meaning the envoy was nonresident. The CSV codes this as 'Envoy Nonresident,' reflecting the nonresident nature of the chief of mission rather than the institutional status of the mission. Both interpretations have merit: 'Legation' reflects the formal establishment of the mission in Belgrade, while 'Envoy Nonresident' reflects the practical reality that the minister was based in Athens. The coding choice depends on whether the dataset tracks institutional mission status (Legation) or the residency status of the chief of mission (Envoy Nonresident). If the dataset convention is to use 'Envoy Nonresident' when the minister is accredited but not resident, the CSV is consistent with that convention.
